content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I used to think that a portfolio was pretty simple. It just needed a projects, a list of skills and a way for people to get in touch with me.
That is what I saw everyone else doing so I did the thing.
Then I looked at a lot of portfolios from other developers and I kept making changes to my own.. Then I figured something out that really changed the way I thought about things.
People who look at portfolios do not just read them they actually experience them.
My portfolio is not a bunch of projects I have done. It is usually the thing that someone looks at before they decide if they want to talk to me. So every little thing matters, like the animations and the way the pages change and how everything is laid out and how people interact with it. All of these things say something about what kind of developer I’m
When I realized this it made me think about my portfolio in a new way. Of just showing what I can do I wanted to create an experience, for people who look at it.
The Problem With Most Portfolios
Let’s be honest most developer portfolios look almost identical. They have a hero section that tells us about the developer. They have a grid that shows their skills. They have a list of projects they have worked on. They have a contact form. This is a structure and it works.
The problem is that every developer uses this structure in the same way. When every portfolio looks the same it is hard for people to remember any of them. After looking at portfolios they all start to look the same. The goal is not to make navigation hard or confuse people with layouts. The goal is to make an experience that feels like it was thought out so it leaves an impression.
Just having projects is not enough. Projects show that you can build software. A portfolio should show how you think. When someone looks at your portfolio they are looking at more than what you can do technically. They are looking for signs of attention to detail and creativity and communication and product thinking and user empathy and problem-solving ability. Your code is on GitHub. Your portfolio tells us the story behind it.
If you design an interaction that’s thoughtful it shows that you care about user experience. If you have a transition it shows that you notice details. If you have a layout it shows that you understand hierarchy. These small things can say more about you than another project. Developer portfolios should be more than a list of projects. They should show us how the developer thinks and what they can do. This is what will make a portfolio stand out and leave an impression on people. Developer portfolios like this will be the ones that people remember. They will be the ones that show us what the developer is really capable of. The developer portfolio is a chance to tell a story and show people what you can do. It is a chance to show people that you are more than a list of skills and projects. You are a developer, with a perspective and way of thinking. Your developer portfolio should show this.
Projects Alone Aren’t Enough
Projects show that you can build software. A portfolio should show how you think. When someone checks your portfolio they’re looking at more than your technical skills.
They’re searching for signs of:
* Attention to detail
* Creativity
* communication
* Product thinking
* Understanding of users
* Problem-solving skills
Your code might be on GitHub. Your portfolio explains it. A designed interaction says, “I care about how users experience things.” A smooth transition says, “I pay attention to things.” A simple layout says, “I know how to organize things.” These small clues often say more, than a list of projects.
Designing an Experience Instead of a Website
The biggest shift happened when I stopped asking myself:
“What sections should I add next?”
Instead, I started asking:
“How should someone feel while exploring this page?”
That one question changed every design decision I made. It really made me think about what I was doing. I stopped trying to fill up all the space on the page. I focused on creating flow. I wanted everything to work together smoothly. Of adding more and more things to the page I tried to reduce friction.
I did not want people to get confused or stuck. I also stopped designing each screen on its own. I designed the journey, between them. I thought about how people would move from one page to the next. Every page became part of a story. It was not something to check off a list. The goal was not to impress people with how complicated the design was. The goal was to make the experience feel effortless. I wanted people to be able to use it quickly. The design question really helped me to make something better. It helped me to make the experience feel easy and simple for people to use.
Motion Should Guide, Not Distract
While experimenting with GSAP, I realized something interesting. Animation isn’t decoration. It’s communication. Good motion helps users understand where to look, what changed, and what action to take next. Instead of throwing animations everywhere, I began using motion to:

• Direct attention

• Create visual hierarchy

• Provide interaction feedback

• Make transitions feel natural
The best animations often go unnoticed because they feel expected. Users shouldn’t think, “Wow, that’s a cool animation.”
They should simply feel that everything flows naturally. When motion serves the user instead of the designer’s ego, it becomes incredibly powerful.
Perspective Changes Everything
One of the most enjoyable experiments in my portfolio involved perspective-based interactions. Rather than relying on flat scrolling, I explored ways to create depth through:

• Layered elements

• Subtle parallax

• Perspective transforms

• Camera-like movement

• Smooth scene transitions
These effects weren’t added to make the website flashy. They were meant to create a stronger sense of immersion. Small amounts of depth make interfaces feel more dynamic without overwhelming users. Just like good cinematography guides viewers through a film, thoughtful perspective can guide visitors through a digital experience.
Thinking Like a Recruiter
Imagine you have to look at one hundred portfolios in one week. After some time they all start to look the same to you. You see the layouts, the same color palettes, the same project cards. Now imagine you open one portfolio. It feels different right away. It does not feel this way because it has a lot of animations or expensive pictures. It feels this way because every part of it seems to have been thought about carefully. This is what I wanted my portfolio to be, like. I did not want it to be flashy or complicated. I just wanted it to be memorable. When someone closes my portfolio I want them to remember how it made them feel not the technologies I used in my portfolio.
Small Details Matter More Than Big Features
One thing I’ve learned is that users rarely remember individual features. They remember the overall experience. That experience is built from dozens of tiny decisions:

• Hover states that provide feedback

• Consistent typography

• Balanced spacing

• Smooth scrolling rhythm

• Thoughtful loading transitions

• Responsive cursor interactions

• Predictable navigation
None of these details are impressive on their own. But together, they create polish. And polish builds trust.
My Biggest Lessons
Building and rebuilding my portfolio has been a great learning experience and I use the lessons I learned from my portfolio in every single project I do. I have found out that having animation does not automatically make the user experience better. The best kind of motion is the kind that has a purpose and actually does something for the user.
I think that simplicity is way harder to achieve than complexity because it takes a lot of work to make something. When I am working on a project I realize that removing things that’re not necessary can be a lot harder than just adding more things to it. The performance of a website matters as much as how it looks because if it is slow it is not very good. If a website is really beautiful but it feels slow then it is still an experience for the user.
Every time I design something I ask myself why I am doing it and what the point of it is. If an animation does not actually make the website easier to use then it probably should not be there because the animation is not doing anything for the user. The design of a website should help the content. Make it easier to understand, not compete with it for the users attention.
My portfolio is meant to show people the work that I do not distract them from it with a lot of stuff. When I am building a website I try to build it for the people who will actually be using it not for the people who will see it on social media. The goal of building a website is not to make something that will be popular for a seconds the portfolio website is meant to be something that people will remember after they close the browser and walk away, from the computer. The goal is to create a website that someone will actually remember. That is what I am trying to do with my portfolio website.
